title: The Sacrifice & the Greek Gift
subtitle: Give material to win the king — and know when the gift is poisoned

concepts.name: Intermezzo (the in-between move)
concepts.short: Before you recapture, look for a more forcing move — a check or threat that must be answered first.

concepts.name: The sacrifice
concepts.short: Give up material for something worth more: a mating attack, a decisive lead in the chase, a winning structure.

concepts.name: The Greek Gift (Bxh7+)
concepts.short: Bishop takes h7 with check; after Kxh7, Ng5+ and the queen joins on h5 to hunt the king.

concepts.name: The exchange sacrifice
concepts.short: Give a rook for a minor piece (the 'exchange') to smash the king's shield or win a decisive attack — material for impact.

concepts.name: Sound vs. unsound
concepts.short: A sacrifice is only good if the defender has no refutation. Check the cool defences before you commit.

lessons.title: First, the In-Between Move
lessons.summary: Don't recapture on autopilot. The intermezzo wins material that a routine recapture throws away.

segments.title: Why recapturing first can be a mistake

intro.text: A sacrifice gives up material on purpose. Before we learn to give material away, we must master the move that lets us KEEP more than our share: the intermezzo. The principle that drives this whole chapter is the same one — the most forcing move comes first.

coach.text: Black has just grabbed your queen on d1. Your hand reaches for Rxd1 — recapture, stay level. Stop. Is there a move MORE forcing than recapturing? Your knight eyes f6, and f6 is a check.

Nxf6+! The check must be answered before Black can save the queen. Black's only replies are ...Bxf6 (the g7-bishop recaptures) or ...Kh8 (stepping aside, leaving my knight on f6). THEN you play Rxd1 — and you have won a whole knight compared with the lazy recapture. The queen on d1 isn't going anywhere; the check is. Forcing move first.

intro.text: Same lesson, your turn. Recapturing immediately is the trap. Find the in-between check.

coach.text: Black took your queen on d1. Don't recapture yet — play the most forcing move on the board.

successLine.text: Nxf6+. Black must answer the check with ...Bxf6 (or step aside with ...Kh8), and only THEN Rxd1 — you come out a clean knight ahead. The check jumped the queue.

retryLine.text: Rxd1 just trades queens and lets Black off. Look for a CHECK first — the knight has one.

intro.text: Each one hides an in-between move. Find the forcing shot before the routine recapture.

lessons.title: The Greek Gift — Bxh7+
lessons.summary: The classic bishop sacrifice on h7 that drags the king out and brings the knight and queen down for the kill.

intro.text: The Greek Gift is the most famous sacrifice in chess. You give a bishop for the h7 pawn — but what you really buy is the enemy king. Here Black has traded his dark-squared bishop and developed the knight to d7 instead of f6. Watch why that matters.

coach.text: Three things make this gift sound. One: my bishop on d3 already stares at h7. Two: my knight on f3 can leap to g5 with check. Three: my queen has a clear road to h5. And crucially — Black has NO knight on f6 to guard h7 or h5, and NO dark-squared bishop to chase my knight off g5. The defences are missing.
Bxh7+! The king has nothing better than to take — declining with ...Kh8 just leaves me a pawn up with the attack still loaded. So ...Kxh7, and the king steps onto the open square where I want him.

Ng5+! The second piece arrives with check. The king must move. To g8 it scuttles back home — but home is no longer safe — or to g6, walking into the open. Either way the knight has done its job: it has fixed the king and opened the door for the queen.

After ...Kg8, the queen lands: Qh5! Qh7# is the threat, and Black can only delay it. ...Qxg5 throws the queen away (then Bxg5 and I am simply winning); the one cool try, ...Nf6 (guarding h7), runs into exf6 — the e5-pawn removes the guard, and Black's king cannot survive the attack that follows. Calculate the defence and you see there is no rescue. The gift has been collected with interest.

intro.text: Same position. You play all three moves of the attack in turn. Forcing move first, every time.

coach.text: Bishop, knight and queen are all aimed at the king, and the f6 and g5 defenders are absent. Launch the gift.

successLine.text: Bxh7+! The king is forced to take — ...Kxh7 — and steps into the storm.

retryLine.text: The gift starts with the bishop. Take h7 with check — Bxh7+.

coach.text: The king took on h7. Bring the second attacker with check.

successLine.text: Ng5+! The king is pried loose again. Now the queen comes.

retryLine.text: A piece sac needs follow-up speed. Jump the knight to g5 — it's a check.

coach.text: The king fled back to g8. Land the queen and threaten mate on h7.

successLine.text: Qh5! Black can only delay Qh7#: ...Qxg5 gives up the queen (Bxg5 and you are winning), and ...Nf6 is met by exf6, when the king cannot survive the attack. That is the whole Greek Gift: bishop, knight, queen — h7, g5, h5.

retryLine.text: The king is on g8 with no guard on h7. Swing the queen to h5 — Qh7# is the threat.

lessons.title: When the King Runs to g6
lessons.summary: If the king tries g6 instead of g8, the h-pawn — not the queen — does the hunting.

intro.text: After Bxh7+ Kxh7 Ng5+, the king has a second choice: ...Kg6, refusing to go home. Now the queen check isn't the cleanest tool — the king is too exposed to a humble pawn.

coach.text: The king stands on g6, out in the open. The killer is h4! — threatening h5+, which forks the king and chases it to its death. The whole point of a sacrifice: you spend the bishop to reach a position this decisive.

After h4, h5+ comes next: if ...Kh6 the knight and queen close a winning net around the king; if the king runs the other way it is hunted down across the board. The lone pawn finishes what the sacrifice began.

intro.text: The king chose g6. Don't reach for a flashy piece — reach for the pawn that traps him.

coach.text: King on g6, fully exposed. Play the quiet pawn move that threatens a deadly check.

successLine.text: h4! Threatening h5+ to drive the king onto a mating net. A sacrifice doesn't always end in a queen check — sometimes a pawn delivers the verdict.

retryLine.text: The king on g6 fears one thing most: h5+. Prepare it — push the h-pawn one square.

intro.text: Real games where giving up material rips the king open. Find the sac and the follow-up.

lessons.title: The Exchange Sacrifice
lessons.summary: Give a rook for a minor piece to tear down the king's shield. The point is never the material — it's the king behind it.

segments.title: Rook for knight — to reach the king

intro.text: The Greek Gift spends a bishop. The exchange sacrifice spends MORE: a rook for a minor piece — that is the 'exchange'. You give roughly two points of material, and in return you rip away the last guard of the enemy king. Same logic as every sacrifice in this chapter: material is the currency, the king is the prize.

coach.text: Look at Black's king: the only thing standing between it and my pieces is the knight on f6. Everything else — queen on a8, bishop on b7, rook will be slow — is far away on the queenside. My rook on f3 can take that knight. Rxf6! A rook for a knight: I hand back two points of material, but I demolish the king's cover.

Black recaptured ...gxf6, and now the king sits in a torn-open shell. Qh6! The queen storms in, and Black's defenders simply cannot get back in time — the position is a forced mating attack (the engine sees mate, and Black's queen on a8 can only watch). I gave the exchange; I collected the king. That is the whole idea: count what the sacrifice BUYS, not only what it costs.

intro.text: Your turn. The f6-knight is the king's only guard, and Black's force is marooned on the queenside. Make the investment.

coach.text: Don't count only the points you lose. The knight on f6 is the last defender — remove it with the rook and the king is yours.

successLine.text: Rxf6! Rook for knight — and after ...gxf6 Qh6 the king has no shield and no help arriving. You spent the exchange and bought a winning attack. That is the exchange sacrifice.

retryLine.text: A rook is worth more than a knight — but a king is worth everything. Take the f6-knight with the rook: Rxf6.

segments.title: Cool-down: pay the exchange, win the king

intro.text: Real games where giving a rook for a minor piece cracks the position open. Spot the sacrifice and the follow-up.

lessons.title: Is the Gift Poisoned?
lessons.summary: The same Bxh7+ that wins one game loses another. Learn the two defenders that refute it before you commit.

intro.text: Viktor's iron rule: a sacrifice is only sound if the defender has NO refutation. Before you play Bxh7+, you check the cool defences. Two pieces, more than any others, poison the gift.

coach.text: Refutation one: the knight on f6. It guards h7, so Bxh7+ isn't even check that wins — and it guards h5, so the queen can never land there. With a knight on f6 the Greek Gift is simply dead. Here White must play quietly; the position is only equal.

Refutation two: the dark-squared bishop on e7. Now if Bxh7+ Kxh7 Ng5+, Black plays ...Bxg5! — handing the bishop back to kill the attacking knight. The fire goes out, and White is simply down material with nothing to show. The cool defence beats the greedy sac.

Back to the sound version. No f6 knight, no e7 bishop — both refutations are absent. THIS is when you give the gift. Same bishop, same h7 square; the difference is entirely in the defenders. Always count the defence before you sacrifice.

intro.text: Here a knight sits on f6. Decide with your move: is the gift on, or do you keep your bishop?

coach.text: The knight on f6 guards both h7 and h5. The gift is poisoned — don't throw your bishop away. Develop the dark-squared bishop to a good diagonal instead and keep the position healthy.

successLine.text: Good — you declined the unsound sac and developed. A sacrifice you can't justify is just a blunder with confidence. Patience here keeps you fully in the game.

retryLine.text: With a knight on f6, Bxh7+ simply loses a piece. Don't sacrifice — develop a piece (the c1-bishop) and keep your material.

lessons.title: Sacrifice to Win
lessons.summary: Mixed sacrifices and attacks, then beat an opponent who has left the door open.

intro.text: Each position rewards giving material to drag the king or a defender out of place. Trust the calculation, then strike.

bossIntro.text: You are White, and you know this position cold. No knight on f6, no bishop on e7 — every defender of the gift is missing. The bishop on d3, the knight on f3, the queen with a road to h5. Give the gift, bring the pieces in the right order, and finish the king. Show me you can sacrifice when it is sound.

intro.text: Finish with attacks that turn material into mate. Forcing move first — always.

options: Always recapturing immediately to stay level on material
A more forcing move — usually a check or a big threat — inserted before you make the 'expected' recapture
A pawn move that opens a file
Castling on the opposite side to your opponent
questions.explain: An intermezzo jumps the queue: you play a forcing move the opponent must answer first, then make your recapture having gained something extra.

questions.prompt: In the Greek Gift, what are the three pieces and the squares they aim at, in order?
options: Rook to h-file, queen to h5, knight to f6
Bishop takes h7, knight to g5, queen to h5
Queen to h5, bishop to g5, rook to h1
Knight to g5, bishop to h7, king to g2
questions.explain: Bxh7+ drags the king out, Ng5+ pries it loose, and Qh5 threatens mate on h7. Bishop, knight, queen — h7, g5, h5.

questions.prompt: Which defender, sitting on f6, makes the Greek Gift (Bxh7+) completely unsound?
options: A pawn on f6
A bishop on f6
A knight on f6 — it guards both h7 and h5
A rook on f6
questions.explain: A knight on f6 covers h7 (so the sac wins nothing) and h5 (so the queen can never arrive). With it on the board, don't sacrifice.

questions.prompt: Viktor's rule for a sacrifice to be sound is:
options: It must win the game within three moves
The defender must have no refutation — you check the cool defences first
You must be ahead in material before you start
It must be a piece, never a pawn
questions.explain: Sound means refutation-proof. Before committing, look for the defender's best reply — the recapture-the-attacker, the cool retreat, the decline — not just one losing line.

questions.prompt: Your rook can recapture the queen on h5 at once — but is there something more forcing first? Find White's best move.
questions.explain: Ne7+! The in-between check must be answered (...Kh7/...Kh8), and only THEN Rxh5 collects the queen — the intermezzo wins more than the routine recapture.

questions.prompt: Black has castled kingside with no knight on f6 and no bishop on e7. What is the most forcing move?
questions.explain: Bxh7+. With both defenders absent the gift is sound: ...Kxh7 walks into Ng5+ and Qh5 with a winning attack.

questions.prompt: The king has just taken your bishop on h7. Play the thematic Greek-Gift continuation.
questions.explain: Ng5+! The second attacker arrives with check, pries the king loose, and clears the road for the queen to reach h5 — the heart of the Greek-Gift attack.

questions.prompt: After the knight check the king fled back to g8. Find White's strongest move.
questions.explain: Qh5! Qh7# is the threat; Black can only delay it (...Qxg5 hangs the queen, ...Nf6 runs into exf6). White is winning.

questions.prompt: This time the king ran to g6, out in the open instead of home. Play the thematic move that traps it (the immediate Qg4+ also wins).
questions.explain: h4! Threatening h5+, which drives the exposed king into a winning net — the thematic 'a pawn finishes the king' idea. The direct Qg4+ is also crushing; both are completely winning.

questions.prompt: White has just played Bxh7+. Black has no knight on f6 and no bishop on e7. Sound sacrifice or blunder?
questions.explain: Both refutations are absent, so after ...Kxh7 Ng5+ and Qh5 give a winning attack. This is exactly when to give the gift.

questions.prompt: White played Bxh7+ here — but Black has a bishop on e7. Sound sacrifice or blunder?
questions.explain: After ...Kxh7 Ng5+ Black plays ...Bxg5!, returning the bishop to kill the attacker. The fire dies and White is simply down material. The e7-bishop refutes the gift.

questions.prompt: Black grabbed the queen on d1; White answered Rxd1, recapturing at once. Best play, or did White miss something?
questions.explain: Recapturing immediately throws away the intermezzo. Nxf6+ first wins a whole knight; after the routine Rxd1 Black equalises with ...Bxe4.

questions.prompt: A knight sits on f6. White still went for Bxh7+. Sound, or a blunder against the defender?
questions.explain: The f6-knight guards h7, so after ...Nxh7 White has simply given up a bishop for a pawn with no attack. Never play the gift into an f6-knight.

questions.prompt: What is an 'exchange sacrifice', and what justifies it?
options: Trading queens to reach an endgame a pawn up
Giving a rook for a minor piece (a knight or bishop) to win a decisive attack or smash the king's shield
Swapping a rook for the opponent's rook on an open file
Giving up a pawn to gain a tempo in the opening
questions.explain: The 'exchange' is rook-for-minor. You spend about two points of material to remove a key defender or open the king — justified when the attack you buy is worth more than the material you give.

questions.prompt: Black's king is guarded only by the knight on f6, and Black's queen and bishop are marooned on the queenside. Find White's winning sacrifice.
questions.explain: Rxf6! The exchange sacrifice — rook for knight. After ...gxf6 Qh6 the king's shield is gone and no defender can return; White has a forced, winning attack. Material spent, king won.
